what_is_seen: The six rear-labeled frames (img#0-5, t≈1708366570.85–1708366595.82) all depict an indoor lab/workshop: suspended ceiling tiles, a ceiling-mounted projector, fluorescent tube, whiteboards with diagrams, shelving with red jerry-can, workbenches, and a handlebar/tripod in the foreground — clearly a static indoor scene with only minor exposure/viewpoint drift. The six left-labeled frames (img#6-10) show an indoor kitchen corner with a red metal chair, broom handles, wall outlet with plug, sink/countertop and a sliver of bright outdoor view through a doorway on the right edge; img#11 (last left frame, t≈1708366595.82) instead shows an outdoor entrance vestibule with glass doors bearing 'LABORATORIO DE INTELIGENCIA ARTIFICIAL Y ROBOTICA' lettering. The six right-labeled frames (img#12-17, t≈1708366570.87–1708366595.84) are consistently outdoor: a sunlit gravel/asphalt parking lot with parked cars (red, blue, silver), trees, grass border, HVAC condensers, brick building facade, and in img#15 three pedestrians with backpacks walking across. Rear and left are therefore indoor while right is outdoor at synchronized timestamps spanning ~25 s.
cross_view_consistency: The right camera corroborates a plausible outdoor parking-lot context throughout the entire 25 s window, including dynamic content (pedestrians appearing in img#15) proving it is a live outdoor feed. Rear is uniformly indoor workshop across all 6 frames with no transition. Left is indoor kitchen for 5 of 6 frames and then switches to an indoor/outdoor vestibule view in the final frame (img#11) — still not a vehicle-rear-view-equivalent scene, and geometrically inconsistent with the parking lot seen on right.
spatial_reasoning: Geometry is physically impossible for a single ego vehicle. The right camera shows an open sunlit parking lot with cars only a few meters away and unobstructed sky/trees; if the vehicle were inside a garage or loading bay (which could explain indoor rear), the right camera could not simultaneously see open sky, grass, and distant parked cars without any wall/door framing. Furthermore the rear 'workshop' scene is static (projector, whiteboards, shelving unchanged) with a tripod/handlebar rig in foreground typical of a bench calibration setup, not a driveable corridor behind a moving platform. The left 'kitchen' scene likewise shows interior cabinetry and a broom leaning on a wall — incompatible with a left-side vehicle mount looking onto the same lot visible on right. The three feeds cannot co-exist on one vehicle at one pose; rear and left must be sourced from stationary indoor cameras.
root_cause_hypothesis: Topic mis-mapping / wrong camera assignment during recording: the /rear and /left image topics were bound to indoor lab monitoring or bench-test cameras (likely a GoPro/webcam on the workshop ceiling facing the whiteboards, and a second camera in the lab kitchen) rather than to the vehicle's rear and left externally mounted sensors. Timestamps are live (pedestrians move on right, exposure varies on rear) so it is not a stale buffer; it is a persistent routing/launch-file misconfiguration affecting this bag from the start of the window (and very likely the entire bag).
data_quality_recommendation: Discard the rear and left image streams for this bag entirely; do NOT use this window for training, calibration, or multi-view evaluation. Re-inspect the launch/remap configuration and verify camera_info serial numbers against topic names before any further recording. Only the right stream may be retained after independent validation. Gate the bag behind a manual QA flag before ingesting into any training pipeline.
safety_impact: High: a perception or localization model trained on these mislabeled streams would learn that 'rear' equals a static indoor workshop and 'left' equals a kitchen, catastrophically corrupting rear-collision and left-side obstacle reasoning; any online use would blind the AV to actual rear/left hazards such as the pedestrians visible on the right camera.
